speaker: interviewer
text: When we were talking about, you know, discussing what had happened to your parents with-- and with other kids discussing it-- you said you got the letter two years later. Whatever happened to the letter? Do still have it, or does your brother have it?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, there's so much stuff we lost.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have any mementos from the-- from your family? Pictures?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing. All I have-- the travel card, or whatever they call that document for entering England, I have that with my picture on it. And my brother, for some-- he's, he's very unhappy. He lost his. You know, living in a home you don't really have your own privacy or anything like that. It's really hard to keep track of anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: So where were we? We were towards the end of the war. Towards the end of the war. And you said how well-organized the British were.

speaker: interviewee
text: Absolutely.

speaker: interviewer
text: That they would evacuate, put you in homes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I give them credit for that. Everything is done. They're calm, and they don't panic. Everything is, yeah, very well-organized, and they move fast. I can imagine if it happened in the United States, it'd have to go through Congress first, I guess. By the time they get done, it's too late to do anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember about, like, rationing? Were times tough?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about that.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, during the war-- well, I don't think the British people starved. There was enough food to go around, but certain things there was a shortage of. There was a shortage of probably sugar, butter, and eggs, and meats, and stuff like-- even candy. I remember we used to have candy rationed, and we'd have these coupons that allowed us to buy I think it was like 12 ounces of candy a month, something like that. And as a kid, it was something that we looked forward to being able to, to enjoy. I don't know if I should talk, too, about the home and how we were treated.

speaker: interviewer
text: Sure. Please.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Coming from a religious background, I don't really like to put down religion because generally speaking, I-- I'm-- I'm-- I respect people who are sincere about religion. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And I don't like to put-- although, you know, we hear religious people who are not good people. And unfortunately, that was the case of, of the supervision, the Jewish home that I was in. His name was Kon, K-O-N, Rabbi Kon. And he was a significant rabbi in the community in Poland. I forget which town-- Lodz or something like that. And him and his family got out before the war, and he abandoned all that-- you know, the position in the community that he enjoyed as being-- that he had. And for him to, to, to end up taking care of little kids, I think he, he, he kind of, you know, resented it, whatever, and he took it out on us. I mean, you would think he should be grateful that he was alive. I mean, him and his whole family survived. He should be-- but no. He was probably always-- probably, I mean, I-- I don't know what was in his mind, but I know he took it out on us. And I just think, probably, that's why he was the way he was. And I think--

speaker: interviewer
text: How would he take it out?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, he would be violent. He would beat the kids. I mean, I was thrashed several times for minor things. I mean, no kid is perfect, and I wasn't perfect. That's for sure. He never touched my brother because my brother wasn't well. And, and I think we were exploited. Not just that, and it wasn't until we were older and able to understand, we saw how we were exploited. He used to send us to deliver eggs and, and butter and God knows what to some other people, or whatever. He was selling our rations. And, and when we were old enough to understand what was going on, some of us started to rebel in a very mild way because we were afraid of him. He was violent. Like I remember one of the-- one of the-- one of the boys, the older boys-- his name was Singer-- he made some kind of comment. I don't even want to say what the wife-- you know, the wife from-- what she did. She spit on him, I mean-- I mean these are people who are supposed to be classy people, you know, in the community and everything, but I was there. I saw it.

speaker: interviewee
text: And but I think we had fear. We feared the man more than anything else. And what can I-- what else do I remember? Were you-- but were you getting enough to eat even though he was selling off your rations?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh. [LAUGHS] I look back now, it was funny. When we were kids, everything we were given was portions. You ever heard the story with Oliver-- not Oliver, what's his name? What's the-- Oliver Twist, that's what I'm trying to think of. You know, Charles--

speaker: interviewer
text: Dickens.

speaker: interviewee
text: --Dickens, and that real classic line, can I have more? I always thought of ourselves being in that position, that a lot of times we couldn't get no more, I mean, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we were getting enough to eat, and that was it. You couldn't just going into the refrigerator or into the cupboard and help yourself because get into trouble, couldn't do that. And so there were times I could've had more food. I would say I was hungry, absolutely. But it didn't show because we, we had nourishment, I guess so. But I think the reason it was limited because whatever access, he'd sell.

speaker: interviewee
text: When we got older, and we had a little more-- you know, we were getting a little more risk-- you know, braver, I remember what we used to do. Like we lived in the multi-story-- this was in London, already. When we came back to London-- after we returned from Ely to London-- and I was probably like 12, yeah, 12-- we would have missions. [LAUGHS] It's funny now you think about it. Especially when they weren't home, we'd have-- we knew where the good stuff was, like the cookies and stuff, you know. And we would set up lookouts at different-- on a different set of staircase and signal if anyone came through. And, yeah, we would steal food. Yeah. I mean, it's pathetic, but we did that. And this was even after the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: Even after the war.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, that was after the war. I mean, I remember to me-- oh, something I didn't mention to you earlier, but this is something that I haven't forgotten yet.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's that?

speaker: interviewee
text: This rabbi, having had this background where he was like a leader of a commune and everything, he used to lecture and teach. He was a teacher. So we were the-- like the-- he used us. He always tried to teach us. I'd rather play soccer. You know, I used to be-- I liked to play outdoors. So, no, I had to come in and listen to him give us these letters-- lessons on the Bible and all that. And he'd go on and on, on. OK, but that isn't so bad as what he used to do. While he was lecturing and teaching us, his family would serve him food. Yeah, and we'd just sit there with our tongues hanging out. And he'd be eating stuff that we never got, like chicken. We never got chicken during the war, or very rarely.

speaker: interviewer
text: Chicken used to be like more scarce than beef in those days. I know things are reversed today, but it was like just for special occasions. And, you know, he'd just chomp away at food while he's talking, and we'd just watch him, you know. It was so common he'd do this. It wasn't always chicken, but sometimes it was eggs. You know, he'd have-- he'd have stuff that we wouldn't get, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: We'd mostly get maybe powder eggs, and he'd have fresh eggs. I mean, you know, it's-- I mean for him to be so insensitive is something that-- you know, the guy is long gone now. I still haven't forgiven him. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: What were you--

speaker: interviewee
text: But that's-- you know, I didn't bring it up before, but yeah, I remember that quite well. That was-- that was so bad, and he did this on a regular basis. You know, it's-- yeah. That was-- [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: What, what were you hearing was going on, you know, one the war scene? What news, what things were you hearing were going on in Europe? Were you hearing about--

speaker: interviewee
text: When? In Ely or in London, it was over.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, in Ely. Back in Ely, when you were--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well--

speaker: interviewer
text: --during the war. Back to Ely. God, it's a lot of memories from Ely. We used to mostly get the information-- once a week we were allowed to go to a movie, OK? And that was like, oh, that was the best thing that we enjoyed, going to a movie. Local theaters-- there were three theaters in Ely, and yeah, we'd go to-- and that was, you know, the best thing that we enjoyed, going to a movie. And this supervisor rabbi, he used to tease the hell out of us before he allowed us to go. We'd have to ask him, and in fact I remember to this day his comment. We'd have to ask him, can we go to a movie tonight? Like let's say it was Tuesday night. We used to go. We used to ask him, can we go to a movie tonight? He'd say, a movie? He'd think he was real-- it was a riot, you know? He'd think he was real funny. He'd say, yeah, you can see a movie in bed, you know. He used to tease the hell out of us. And when he did let us go to the movie, of course, that's-- in those days every movie had a news reel, like 15, 20 minutes, and it gives you a pretty good idea. It was not commercial. Like here, news is commercial. It was strictly documentary, and it would update you what's going on in the war, as far as what's going on.

speaker: interviewee
text: And of course radio. Radio-- religiously you would listen to radio news, always. There was TV there, but radio was very, very-- everybody listened to news, always.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you hearing about the camps and ghettos or--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, nothing at all until after the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you didn't know that there were concentration camps?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I think it was-- I found out afterwards. It was-- it was known, but it was-- it never seemed to have been an issue. Like it was-- the news media didn't seem to do a much about it, yet it was no secret. I understand it started-- a concentration camp started way back before the war, you know, in Germany and places like that, not in Czechoslovakia.

speaker: interviewer
text: But Hitler started his concentration camps way before the war. It's just the world didn't seem to make an issue of it for some reason. The news media didn't. And I don't know. Politics, I guess. I mean, even Chamberlain tried to appease Hitler, didn't he? You know, he offered Czechoslovakia as a gift, like it was his to give Czechoslovakian to, to Germany to appease Hitler. What did they-- what did you-- what did the kids think about it? I mean, obviously, hearing what was going on, and, you know, were there kids that wanted to go sign up? Obviously, there were older kids there, too. Did any of them--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, many of them volunteered, yeah. The older kids, yeah, absolutely.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you keep in touch with them at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they were older. But-- oops. [LAUGHS] They were older, and they would come back when they had some time off-- come back in uniform. And it was always-- as a kid I remember, oh, I loved to see them come back in uniform, you know, and ask them about their experiences and that.

speaker: interviewer
text: What would they tell you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, one guy was in Italy. He was a driver, driving in the mountains in a, you know, transport in Italy. And there was Otto Steiner. He was telling me about the good times with the French girls. You know, he was kind of a ladies man. [LAUGHS] I didn't tell-- I didn't hear much about action from them as far as from the war. Maybe they didn't talk about it, or they weren't involved maybe. Not every soldier gets involved in combat.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the ones that went from the boys home, I don't remember them telling us anything about combat. The closest one that I know is the driver because, you know, he would drive and sometimes get attacked, I guess. But, you know, war zones-- shipping supplies and stuff. How did you hear about the war ending? Do you remember--

speaker: interviewee
text: About the what?

speaker: interviewer
text: When the war ended, where were you? Ho do you remember hearing about it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was in Ely. And if you can imagine, the world was black, dark. There was no streetlights, no nothing, and that was a big change is, is as soon as the war was over, the lights went on in the streets and everything. Well, there was lights in the houses, but everything was black out. And they had inspectors go by, and if they see any light, like it wasn't perfect, they'd knock your door and say, hey, we can see some light in that window. Fix it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, why did they want to-- why weren't they allowing light?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because of airplanes. Airplane-- you know, because that could be seen from, from, from the air. One thing that's real-- this house that we lived in, in Ely, can you imagine how old that house was? It was still piped for gas lighting. [LAUGHS] That every room had, had-- we didn't use it because we had electricity already, but it still had the pipes in the wall for gas lighting. Every room in the house had a fireplace, every room. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So you had to turn out all your lights at night.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yeah. Yeah. Well, well, it had to be blacked out, yeah. That was-- that was a very unusual building we were in, old.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, where were you when you heard war ended?

speaker: interviewee
text: The exact location, I don't remember, but I was in Ely. I don't remember if I was in school, if we were at the home, but it was a happy day, you know. Everybody relieved, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened? What did you do that day?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember. I don't remember. What I didn't mention earlier, that during this whole time we were living in this home, we didn't have any money. We had-- like when we went to a movie, the cost of that movie was the most of our pocket money that we used to get. We got no other money.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you're given like an allowance.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, we had an allowance-- pocket money, they called it-- a week, and it was just enough to pay for a movie, and a few pennies left. That was it. So when you say-- well, at the age of 12, I'm not going to go out and get drunk. I mean, I-- I mean, there's-- I don't know what we did. I'm saying I didn't have any money to do anything anyway. [LAUGHS] But I remember it was a great moment. It was definitely a great moment, yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was-- it was not easy, the war time. There was all these-- I don't know how many times during these air raids, I always remember my stomach going crazy, you know? There was always that fear. How soon after the war ended did you go back to London?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I think I mentioned earlier, we were probably the last people to go back-- to go back to, to London because they had to find a new location to relocate the boys home. And so it was probably almost a year afterwards. Oh, what was interesting is in Ely, I remember there was only three schools. There was our school, King School who allowed us to use their premises, then there was-- god, what's the name of the other school? There was one other public school that was local, non-Jewish, and there was constant friction between our school and, and the non-Jewish school-- I mean, like fights and stone-throwing. It was a prejudice thing, but I don't understand it. In Ely, there wasn't a Jew in that place till we came there. And they thought Jews were people with horns or something, so that was their upbringing.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that was why there was this anti-Semitism. You know, kids it's easy for them to do. Anyway, the point I want to make is that when everybody went back to London, the school just closed down-- the Jewish school-- and we a-- we stayed on, and we had to go to school. And guess what? We had to go to this school that we feared. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: And because of my age, I had to go in a class all by myself. There was no other kids because I was younger than my brother. The other kids were mostly my brother's age and one year older. So they-- so the few of us that were left, there was least three of them in a class or whatever. I was alone, and I'm telling you, I was scared.

speaker: interviewee
text: And you know something? I was there maybe one semester. At the end of semester I had the best friends there. Because they get to know--

speaker: interviewee
text: They got to know me. I was accepted. I was invited to their house. It's like fear is what, what, what generated that, that hate and anti-Semitism. And also, the British-- if you're good at sports, you're their hero, and I was the best, [LAUGHS] you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you play?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, everything. Soccer, cricket. I was-- later on in high school in London I was captain of the soccer team, captain of the cricket team. I mean, you know, I was always tops in sports. And boy, they respect you. You're like-- you know. And I mean, that was my foot in to their acceptance, I guess. And I was very popular with them, and they invited me to their houses. And remember one time, in fact, by accident-- I swung a bat, and there was a kid standing where he wasn't supposed to stand, one of the kids in the class. I smashed him right in the mouth. I split his lip. I felt awful. And it wasn't my fault, and you know, when he came back to school after he had it stitched up and everything, he-- I'm trying to think-- yeah, he almost apologized to me that I felt bad. He said, it wasn't your fault.

speaker: interviewer
text: But when you first went in, and you said you were the only one in your age group--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, it was scary.

speaker: interviewer
text: How they treat you? What happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, distant. It was uncomfortable in the beginning. And oh, another thing-- I was never that good in school because I had other-- my brother was great. I was well, maybe in the top third, but I was never-- didn't stand out because I never really gave it a full effort. Because I always put most of my energy in sport and art. Those were my two subjects I was really good in. And all the subjects I had to do, you know, so I didn't really put a full effort. But in this school, the teacher had like a couple of pets that were top in the class and all that. I ended up, I was not only first in the exam-- never in my life, in any school, was I top, number one-- I was not first in the exam. And they also do an overall term's work. I was top in term-- you know, overall term's work. And I overheard the teacher take these two guys and give them a lecture. How could you let this guy go ahead of you, and all that. You know, he's new here and everything else. See, I remember that. It's not that I'm that-- I was never that good of a student, but I think I was kind of driven to make an extra effort because of my situation, because normally I just didn't give it that much effort.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you still keep in touch with any of the boys from the Ely home? Or did you keep in touch with them after?

speaker: interviewee
text: There's one guy. He was like my idol, because he was older and I thought he was just a nice guy. His name is Benno Katz. He lives in, in West LA somewhere. I kept in touch with him in Israel, because he went to Israel. And there's one, Steiner. He's in Israel. But I kind of lost track with him, you know. Let's see, what else? Trying to think.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, a year-- it took them a year to find housing for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. About that. Now, in London I went-- for some reason I ended up going-- the home was given two scholarships to a private, orthodox school. Don't ask me why they sent me and this other kid. I was the last one who was a suitable candidate, because I was kind of a little bit rebellious, I guess. But yeah, I went to the orthodox school, which I hated because I had all these religious studies in addition to normal studies. Plus, it cut into my sport activities. I didn't have that much time for that. There's one friend, very close friend that I made in that school, that I'm in touch with to this day. His name is Leon. He comes from a big family, and I knew his whole family. And I think, let's see-- one, two-- about four of his family used to go to that school, and I know them all. But with him, I've kept in touch.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he lives--

speaker: interviewee
text: He lives in London, yeah. He still lives in London.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your brother went-- so you and your brother were split up now. You were in the orthodox school.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, he went to regular public school. He had everything I wanted. [LAUGHS] He had all the sports, and he didn't care for sports. And they had an indoor swimming pool in his school, wow. He had all the things that I would have liked to have had.

speaker: interviewer
text: After he finished school, he went to training up north England, a place called Thaxted, where they had-- oh, we joined Bnei Akiva, which was a Jewish Zionist group, OK? And after that-- it was like a continuation of that-- he went to farm training in Thaxted, up north.

speaker: interviewee
text: He lived there, and he was trained, preparing to live on a kibbutz. And that was the first time my brother was happy. And he loved it over there-- the community over there, such a friendly environment, out with the other kids. And also, him being-- like, he wasn't a well child-- he got better being in that environment. He became stronger and healthier. And somewhere along the line, we joined-- we signed up for Youth Aliyah, which is immigration to Israel-- youth immigration to Israel. And that's how I got to Israel because that's one of things we always wanted to do is go to Israel. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you sign up for it, and when did you eventually get there? How long did it take?

speaker: interviewee
text: That I don't remember. Probably-- let's see we were in England in '46. We were in England three years. It probably took a year or two, yeah. And I was always-- my dream was always to go to study art, you know, go to get into an art school. And in England it's very difficult to get in because they have so many people waiting. I finally got in on a trial basis at a school in London called Camberwell School of Arts and Crafts, and yeah, that was the best school ever went to. I loved-- that was-- I mean, I can't tell you how much I loved going to that school. I mean, I never waited for the bell to ring to go home. I was probably always the last one there. Something I always wanted to do, and I finally made it to that school. After trying, and interviews, and interviews, I finally was accepted.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're going to change tape, and then we're going pick up with the art school.
